scenario: Northpeak Bank, a regional retail bank, processes 8000 customer onboarding requests per month through Camunda 8.8 Forms. The onboarding form has a single text field whose Key is set to the path user.info.age, and an applicant enters the value 21, expecting the runtime to persist that value within 2 seconds of form submission so a downstream service task can read the applicant age.
question: What variable structure does the runtime produce on submission of that field?

options.text: A single flat process variable literally named user.info.age with the value 21 stored under that exact dotted string
A nested process variable structure where 21 lives under user, then info, then age inside the resulting submission JSON
A nested process variable structure where 21 is stored under user.age while the middle info segment is dropped silently
An array process variable named user whose first element places 21 under an info key at index zero of that array list

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime interprets dots in the Key as a path into nested objects rather than as part of a literal variable name, so the dotted string is not used as a single flat variable identifier.

b.text: Correct. A path key such as user.info.age tells the runtime to build a nested object structure, so a submitted value of 21 lands under user, then info, then age in the resulting JSON.

c.text: Incorrect. The runtime walks every segment of the path key when building the output, so no segment is dropped; all three of user, info, and age appear in the resulting nested structure.

d.text: Incorrect. Path keys produce nested objects rather than arrays; an array output would require a dynamic list with its own path configuration, not a simple path key on a single field.

explanation: A key that contains dots is treated as a path into a nested object. Setting a field with key user.info.age to the value 21 yields a JSON object where user contains info, which in turn contains age set to 21, exactly as described under Simple key vs path key.

docs.title: Forms data binding - simple key vs path key
